chapters.title: Never trust the wrapper
chapters.text:
Business is always a gamble. Some weeks the dice roll your way, and other weeks you wonder why you ever walked through the door. When a company catches fire and money starts pouring in, the noise around it makes sense.

WeWork was once one of the most valuable names in the business world. Adam Neumann founded the coworking giant in 2008, and at its peak the company was worth a stunning forty seven billion dollars. Then, in September 2019, the whole thing fell apart. Around forty billion dollars of value evaporated in days, a fall almost no one had ever seen.

Investors and employees woke up to a nightmare, watching a so-called sure thing collapse in front of them. Neumann, by some strange twist, walked away with most of his fortune. His reputation was a different story.

The picture that came out was not flattering. Neumann loved the high life, and he spent freely at every chance he got. As history has shown, lavish spending rarely ends with a happy ending.

Neumann had spotted a craving in a new generation. Younger workers were tired of dull, characterless office cubicles, and they wanted a space that felt more like home. WeWork answered that call. The company rented out offices, dressed them up with playful design, and offered perks that rivaled top hotels. Growth came fast.

By 2019, WeWork was running four hundred and twenty five locations worldwide, and the brand was easy to spot in almost any major city.

Almost no one saw how much money the company was burning, even the people investing in it. The losses were not the result of a bad plan or a broken model. They were the result of Neumann's appetite for spending. He lived like a king, with a personal net worth near ten billion dollars, seven homes, and a small army of staff that followed him from place to place.

Where did it all go wrong? This summary walks through the surprising story of WeWork's rise and crash, and the biggest lesson is the old one. Never judge a book by its cover, because the story inside can be far darker than the shine on the dust jacket.

chapters.title: Every empire starts with a small seed
chapters.text:
Adam Neumann was never the type to settle. He was born to Israeli parents in April 1979, and his parents split up when he was seven. He moved to the United States with his mother and sister, though the new life was rough and the family eventually returned to Israel.

Back home, Neumann grew bored with his small town and started dreaming of something bigger. He left mandatory military service, told his sister to pack a bag, and the two of them set off for New York City.

At twenty seven, Neumann launched a small company called Krawlers, selling baby clothes. The star product was a onesie and pant set with built-in knee pads, designed to help babies crawl. What he did not yet see was the obvious flaw in the plan. Babies crawl for only a few months before they start walking, and the market for kneepads disappears almost overnight.

New York City was magnetic for Neumann, since the city felt full of open doors. He was drawn to the bright lights and the fast pace.

Even with the rough start, Neumann was making money, and his natural hustle kept him pushing deals with suppliers. New ideas kept landing in his lap, and he floated from gig to gig for a while, even giving modeling a try.

In the summer of 2005, Neumann met the man who would become his business partner, Miguel McKelvey. Where Neumann was loud and quick, McKelvey was quiet and thoughtful. They batted ideas back and forth and settled on becoming office space providers. The plan was loose, and the details were still missing, until Neumann met Jack Guttman, a landlord with a spare floor. Neumann worked his charm, and Guttman agreed to come along.

As McKelvey said later, Neumann had a bottomless faith in his ability to talk people into his way of thinking.

Once Guttman said yes, the partners sketched out the layout and design. Neumann wanted a community feel in the offices, and the early signs were good. MTV rented one of the first spaces. They called the new venture GreenDesk.

chapters.title: WeWork caught a shift in how people wanted to work
chapters.text:
GreenDesk grew stronger, and Neumann started to feel restless. He wanted more, and a small win was never enough. The result was WeWork.

Neumann had noticed a clear shift in the workforce. Millennials were walking into offices for the first time, and they wanted something different. They did not want characterless cubicles. They wanted a social feel, a place that reflected how they saw their work. Neumann set out to build a workspace that felt closer to a college dorm than a corporate floor.

Around this time, Neumann met Rebekah, who would become his wife. Things were lining up, and he started to pitch his WeWork idea. The pitch was bold, but it landed, especially with the early success of GreenDesk behind him.

Joel Schriber, an investor with a sharp eye, decided to write a check, and the pace picked up. GreenDesk was folded into WeWork to fund new space, and Neumann began knocking on doors of real estate agents. Schriber even signed the guarantee on the first lease, though the rent was steep, and Miguel McKelvey had to keep calling Schriber to chase the promised payments. Meanwhile, Rebekah Neumann started shaping the brand's look and feel.

The first WeWork building, which also served as the company headquarters, opened in the SoHo district of New York City.

Success came at a sprint. As Neumann brought on more staff, he handed out stock options, which is common in tech startups. Instead of walking new hires through the business model, he told them how much their shares would be worth once the company hit a five hundred billion dollar valuation. For Neumann, the question was never if, only when.

The company spread fast through 2012 and 2013, with new spaces in Manhattan, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

A side note: WeWork rode the wave of remote work and the focus on work life balance, both of which fueled demand for flexible office space.

chapters.title: The first cracks showed up in the ethics
chapters.text:
From day one, Adam Neumann pushed his team hard. He told new hires they would need to make personal sacrifices and work late, sometimes over the weekend. He framed the work as a shared mission, a project the whole company would win together.

Behind the scenes, though, Neumann was cutting deals that lined his own pockets. He had told the company at the start that he would not take a salary, but he soon began pulling large chunks of cash out of the business and moving them straight into his personal accounts. His lifestyle was growing faster than the business, and he needed more and more money to fund it.

Most tech startups, the bucket WeWork was still trying to fit into, did not follow that pattern. The usual rule is that employees and investors start to see gains at the same time. The founder becoming extremely rich before the rest of the team is considered a bad sign.

Not long after WeWork took off, people started asking whether the company was really a tech company or whether it had drifted into real estate.

Neumann began closing deals that made him money from the same buildings WeWork was leasing. He used company cash to fund the investments, and the more successful the deals became, the more he poured in. The conflicts of interest were obvious, and many inside the company worried about how it looked to outside investors. A long list of self dealing deals is a quick way to scare off new money.

Neumann shrugged off the warnings, even from his inner circle, who told him that using WeWork's money to buy real estate was a bad look.

All the while, his personal life grew louder. He was often spotted at celebrity parties, flashing cash, and buying homes around the world. He enjoyed the perks of being the face of one of the most talked about startups, and his charm often got him out of tight spots. That reputation for antics was starting to follow him.

chapters.title: Never spend more than the till can cover
chapters.text:
Neumann's high cost lifestyle was starting to dent WeWork's books, and it was clear that the company was spending far more than it was bringing in.

Most tech startups start in the red, and after about seven years they either break even or watch the losses shrink. WeWork was moving in the opposite direction. The losses were growing, and yet a lot of people inside the company were looking the other way.

Life was getting harder for startups in Silicon Valley at the time. Many tech companies had boomed in their first years, only to start reporting heavy losses. WeWork looked like the loudest example of the pattern.

The basic problem was simple. The money chasing companies had grown much faster than the number of strong ideas.

Keen to steady the books, Neumann went looking for fresh investment. The biggest play was a major deal with SoftBank, and the valuation on that deal was about twenty times the company's actual revenue.

Another move was to start subleasing space to large companies. The plan worked at first, and Amazon became one of WeWork's first big tenants. Still, costs kept climbing, and revenue did not keep up. The growth was moving faster than the cash.

Fast growth is not always a gift. A company that scales too quickly often loses control of quality.

Neumann refused to admit the problem. He did not want to spell out the need for more money, since that would dent the optimistic story. He also refused to share the company's actual numbers, even though full disclosure could have attracted new investors. The trouble was that the numbers, once shown, would scare most investors off.

The SoftBank deal fell through, which was a heavy blow. Neumann's inner circle started pushing him to open the books and go public. They needed cash, and they needed it soon.

chapters.title: The WeWork collapse sent shockwaves around the world
chapters.text:
Almost no one outside Neumann's inner circle knew the trouble WeWork was in. Neumann himself was still in denial, and he kept up the party lifestyle, traveling with celebrity friends and investing in their side projects, including a company tied to Ashton Kutcher.

At the January 2019 WeWork Global Summit, Neumann looked worn out. His usual energy was nowhere to be seen, and the strain of the failed SoftBank deal was visible.

WeWork's spending was far above its revenue. In 2018, spending hit an estimated three and a half billion dollars, while revenue sat at one and a half billion.

The board decided that going public was the best shot at fresh capital. The plan meant filing for an Initial Public Offering, an IPO, which lets public investors buy shares and pour money into the company. To go public, the company had to file a handbook for potential investors, which laid out what the company was and how it was run. The handbook became a problem.

Even after nine years in business, WeWork still could not say clearly what it was. The numbers were hard to present, and the back office looked messy. Investors grew nervous, and the argument over tech startup versus real estate firm kept getting louder.

At the same time, stories about WeWork's corporate governance started to leak. Public valuation began to slide, the drop was small at first, and then the bottom fell out.

The board started asking harder questions. Neumann's long spell of charm was wearing off, and he could feel a coup forming. Pressure to step down kept building.

The company's valuation crashed to a record low, and Neumann was pushed out as CEO. SoftBank bought the company at a steep discount in March 2020. In the thirty three days after the IPO filing, WeWork's value dropped by seventy percent, and investors lost a fortune.

Adam Neumann's charm could not save him from a failure of that scale.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
No business is safe from failure, and even a strong idea can collapse under poor leadership. A solid foundation and steady hands at the top make success far more likely.

WeWork had a great idea and a workable model, but the leadership and the back office let it down. Instead of pouring cash into the business and building for the long haul, Adam Neumann chased a fast and lavish life. Celebrity parties, exotic vacations, multiple homes, and heavy spending are expensive hobbies, and they were funded by money pulled out of the company.

The core idea was sound. Millennials care a lot more about work life balance than earlier generations, and coworking spaces are perfect for remote workers and small teams that want a more social workplace. So the concept was never the problem. The problem was the way the company was run. Was it a tech startup, or was it a real estate operation with shaky leadership, as so many suspected?

Many investors lost a lot of money when WeWork collapsed, and many employees lost their jobs. As of 2022, Adam Neumann's personal net worth was about one and a half billion dollars. For such a stunning fall from grace, Neumann still managed to land on his feet, while the people who worked with and for him paid the highest price.

Try this. In business and in your own finances, it pays to spend less than you earn, so build a budget and stick to it. Living large feels exciting, but it often leads to burnout, so leave room for the quieter moments. If you have a business idea, follow the plan and watch your cash flow closely.

Read it till the end?
